data.title: Fidelity Contrafund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the fourth quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 2.61%, versus 2.66% for the benchmark, the S&P 500® index. Stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace, supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. The past three months, we continued to favor fast-growing, "best of breed" firms. Importantly, given our focus, longer-term performance comparisons strongly favor the fund over the benchmark.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. Equities rose nonetheless, boosted by earnings momentum and a constructive expansionary backdrop. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting, as it looked to balance a recent slowdown in hiring and inflation running higher than its 2% target. The S&P 500® rose 2.34% for the month.

Momentum faded a bit in November (+0.25%), as stocks weathered the shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off that mostly reflected concern about an AI bubble. However, the focus returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index achieved a record close on Christmas Eve before a sluggish final week resulted in a flattish gain for the month, a 17.88% rise for the year and a 38.65% increase since the low on April 8.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks were up 9%.

All other groups lagged the index for the three months. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%), small components of the S&P 500, each lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but weakness (-1%) among insurers, and energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Security selection in the capital goods industry within the industrials sector notably contributed to the fund's performance versus the S&P 500® in Q4. This mostly reflected a sizable non-benchmark stake in Space Exploration Technologies (+89%), a private company that makes spacecraft and provides satellite communications and launch capabilities. We like SpaceX, in part, for its Starlink business focusing on low-earth-orbit satellites, which has created a web of broadband internet access to previously underserved communities in rural and remote areas. In December, investors applauded reports of the potential for an initial public offering in 2026.

In tech, largely avoiding Oracle (ORCL) contributed to relative performance. In Q4, shares of the software and cloud-services provider returned about -31%, so we made a good decision in eliminating the fund's scant exposure in November. We were skeptical about the profitability of Oracle's cloud business relative to peers, including Amazon.com (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Conversely, our stock picks in media & entertainment stood out as the largest detractor versus the benchmark, especially overweights in Meta Platforms (META) (-10%), parent of Facebook and Instagram, and video-streaming service provider Netflix (NFLX) (-22%).

In October, Meta reported record quarterly revenue but net income that was much lower than expected, largely due to spending on AI infrastructure. The company said it expects total expenses to "grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026 than 2025." We like that Meta has embraced AI to improve the quality and relevance of the content and advertisements it shows its users. We reduced exposure to Meta the past three months, but the stock remained by far the top holding and overweight at year-end.

Shares of Netflix fell due to weaker-than-expected Q3 earnings and an announced plan to buy Warner Bros. Q3 revenue growth was in line with guidance, but operating margin missed due to a tax dispute with Brazil that cut into earnings. The stock fell further in December after Netflix announced the proposed Warner Bros. purchase, an acquisition that would include Warner's film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO. Investors worried the deal could face regulatory scrutiny. We lowered the fund's stake in Netflix in Q4, but it remained a large holding and overweight, based on its leadership position and the stickiness of its business.

Outlook and Positioning

As 2026 begins, the global and U.S. business cycles remain constructive, with monetary and fiscal easing in the U.S. likely to continue, in our view. Noteworthy trends in the U.S. include strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and wealth-powered high-end consumption. Capital spending by corporations has experienced a powerful AI-driven upswing, although signs of broadening activity remain faint. We believe the massive tax-and-spending package that became law on July 4 provides an additional tailwind for corporate earnings growth.

The Fed cut its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25% percentage points in September, October and December, amid signs of softer employment conditions. The market and Fed expect two more rate cuts in 2026, due to expectations for soft labor markets and short-lived inflation. By December, central banks in the eurozone, the U.K. and Canada were all on pause.

The near-term outlook appears favorable for continued economic and business expansion, but we believe policy uncertainty, inflation persistence and elevated asset valuations bear watching. Uncertainty related to economic policy dropped in the second half of 2025 after hitting a record high in April, but it has remained elevated and stands in sharp contrast to stock-market volatility, which declined back to roughly its historical average.

The U.S. dollar dropped roughly 10% against major currencies in 2025, its worst calendar year since 2017. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields finished 2025 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%, completing a fairly range-bound year.

After two years of near-zero goods inflation, tariff hikes led to a rise in goods prices in the second half of 2025. Inflation remained sticky in housing and services, keeping headline consumer inflation elevated and notably higher than the Fed's 2% target.

In managing the fund, we follow the investing tenet that a stock's price follows the actual and expected earnings per share of the underlying company over time. So, as the earnings outlook for the fund's holdings changed in Q4, we made only modest adjustments.

Combining technology and communication services, the fund is overweight what we consider tech-related "growth plays." The embrace of AI by companies, governments and individuals is the biggest trend in the market, in our view. We believe AI will affect every business, so we are working closely with the Fidelity research team to identify winners and losers in this changing tech landscape. Accordingly, the fund's position in communication services, including sizable commitments to Meta, Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL) and Netflix, represented 22% of assets and was by far the top sector overweight. Meta is classified as a media name, but we view it as a leading, founder-led tech firm that has embraced AI to improve two critical parts of its business: choosing content and ads to show users, and helping advertisers create better ads.

Tech represented 26% of assets at year-end, the top sector allocation and essentially unchanged from September 30. The biggest holdings here are AI-focused chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA), software and cloud giant Microsoft, personal-electronics giant Apple, and Amphenol, which makes electronic and fiber-optic connectors.

The fund's allocation to the financials sector was about 16%, a bit lower than three months ago but still a meaningful overweight. Insurance-focused conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) anchors the fund's position in the sector, followed by much smaller investments in banking firm JPMorgan Chase and credit-card processor Visa.

We continue to seek more exposure to international stocks and small- and mid-cap firms. The latter are often new issues from the past five to seven years. We believe many of these businesses, which we call "tomorrow's blue chips," can grow faster than the largest companies in the market.

Fidelity® Contrafund® (FCNTX)

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Contrafund® is an opportunistic, diversified equity strategy with a large-cap growth bias.

Philosophically, we believe stock prices follow companies' earnings, and those companies that can deliver durable multiyear earnings growth provide attractive investment opportunities.

As a result, our investment approach seeks firms we believe are poised for sustained, above-average earnings growth that is not accurately reflected in the stocks' current valuation.

In particular, we emphasize companies with "best of breed" qualities, including those with a strong competitive position, high returns on capital, solid free-cash-flow generation and management teams that are stewards of shareholder capital.

We strive to uncover these investment opportunities through in-depth bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.

data.title: Charter Communications Defies Fears Of Extinction
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications (CHTR) have been a terrible performer over the past year, losing nearly half of their value. Concerns about degradation of the broadband business, combined with its debt load, are the primary challenge facing shares. Broadband was once seen as a growth segment, immune from the cord-cutting of video, after all, we all need internet for streaming, etc. However, increased competition from nontraditional players and slow household formations have pressured this business. Strong Q4 results may temper these fears a bit, and shares surged nearly 8% in response in early trading. I have argued these fears are overblown, most recently rating CHTR a “strong buy” in November but being bullish on Charter was one of my worst calls in 2025. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit CHTR to determine whether shares can rebound in 2026 or if the pain will continue.

Cost cuts support EBITDA

In the company’s fourth quarter, Charter earned $10.34, which was $0.46 ahead of expectations even as revenue declined by 2.4% to $13.6 billion. EBITDA declined by 1.2% to $5.7 billion with the decline primarily due to lower political advertising on its local networks. Advertising was down $139 million, and this decline is entirely expected given the Presidential election last year, which boosts spending; the midterms this year should provide a boost to ad revenue.

Absent this, EBITDA would likely have been slightly higher, given the company’s very strong cost controls. Thanks to growth in mobile, Charter has been able to push one-product customers to two product relationships, though it continues to see losses of 3+ product customers, given the ongoing pressure in video, though there are hopeful signs this may be changing.

Broadband attrition is stable, video is encouraging

Broadband customers declined by 119,000, so it does remain a challenging environment for this segment; the pace of decline is better than the 177k loss a year ago. The Q4 customer loss was similar to what we have seen the prior six months. While results here are not strong, with shares so beaten up, it has allowed investors to sigh a breath of relief that the situation has not worsened. Internet customers are down 1.3% from last year. Thanks to pricing increases, revenue was up 0.7% from last year to $5.9 billion. I expect 0.2-0.7% growth in internet revenues in 2026 as price increases offset a continued modest bleed in customers.

Mobile remains a strong spot with CHTR adding 428k lines and approaching 12 million customers. Total customers are up 19% from last year, but I expect the pace of growth to moderate going forward, given it has already captured a substantial portion of its own customer base. I expect growth to narrow towards 10-12% by the end of 2026. Mobile revenue was up 13%, and it may pass $1 billion in Q1 2026 (if not then, it should in Q2). This is a small part of the business at ~7% of revenue, so its growth has a limited impact. Still, mobile is steadily becoming a more important driver of financial results.

When I last wrote about Charter, I discussed my view that after years of attrition video may be approaching its bottom, as most of the remaining customers are likely loyal to the product and CHTR’s packaging of streaming services has improved the value of its cable proposition. Evidence is mounting this may be the case. It added 44k in Q4, and its pace of annual declines has narrowed to 2.2%. Perhaps, video is not in an unstoppable decline after all.

Still, while customers are stabilizing, all is not good in this business. Video revenue was down 10% to $3.2 billion. Its added streaming offerings reduced revenue by about $125 million, or ~4%. All else equal, I do expect this business to decline, and the need to invest in more content to maintain subscribers is likely to reduce margins, but the pace of decline is improving. I expect revenue to decline mid single-digits here in 2026.

Cash flow trends are solid

Importantly, while revenue clearly has pressure, Charter is a very cash generative business. Free cash flow was $5 billion, up $700 million from last year. Declines in cap-ex should support meaningful free cash flow growth even in a flat revenue environment. In 2025, CHTR focused that free cash flow on shareholders, repurchasing $5.4 billion of stock, including $760 million in Q4. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 11% from last year.

In 2026, Charter plans to invest $11.4 billion in cap-ex. This is down about $300 million from last year as it starts to complete network expansion efforts. Importantly, we should see cap-ex continue to decline as it completes the network evolution. I remain unconvinced that cap-ex can really sustainably decline to $7.5-$8 billion. I view $9.5-10 billion as the more likely ending place, given inflation. Still, this will unlock ~$2 billion of free cash flow relative to 2025 levels. With this clear cash saving, free cash flow is likely to rise over time even if we do see ~3-5% revenue declines, a meaningful deterioration of its flat to down 1% trend.

The company ended the year with $477 million of cash and about $95 billion of debt. The absolute size of its debt load is clearly high, and this has been a source of concern for investors. However, relative to Charters’s earnings power, the debt load is sustainable. The company ended the year with 4.2x leverage, stable over the past year. After it completes its acquisition of Cox, Charter aims to bring leverage to a targeted range of 3.5-4.0x, given its larger scale and Cox’s own solid balance sheet. This will be a two-year process, and I expect leverage to remain with 0.2x of current levels through 2027. Given its fairly predictable revenue and strong free cash flow, I view this as safe place to run the business. At current levels, I would much rather see CHTR spend free cash flow on buybacks than on debt reduction.

Shares are very undervalued

Overall, Charter reported a solid Q4. We saw similar underlying trends to recent results, and the pace of pressure on the business is not worsening. Video is also becoming a source of some upside surprise. The ability to enhance the value of this product also should give comfort that CHTR is not a slave to declines in broadband either and can invest to improve the product and customer retention.

At $200, shares have a 17% free cash flow yield, which speaks to how negative sentiment has become. As discussed above, this is not a melting ice cube business, rather free cash flow is set for growth. I expect at least $5 billion again this year, and over $6.2 billion of stand-alone free cash flow in 2027. Even at a 12% free cash flow yield to protect investors against a 2% long-term decline in the business and still yield a 10% long-term return, that points to fair value in the $340 area. As such, I continue to see Charter as a strong buy.

While ongoing negativity around the business may make this convergence to fair value a very prolonged process, I see CHTR as tremendously undervalued. Sentiment is far too negative given actual cash flow trends, and I believe shares can at least recover to $300 this year. I view it as a compelling value and expect 2026 to be a much better year.

data.title: TechCreate Stock Soars 2,500% In 5 Days, Here's What We Know
data.description:
TechCreate Group overview

For those who have just had TechCreate Group Ltd. (TCGL) appeared on their radar this week, they are a Singapore-based technology company that mainly provides digital payment systems, banking platforms, cybersecurity, and some other cloud solutions. But as you definitely saw, the stock experienced a hefty surge, jumping about 890% yesterday and then rising about 140% so far in pre-market trading today. It was a massive stock bump, taking shares from the $8 range they were at the start of this week to the $206 range today.

As you would expect following this kind of unusual trading activity, the company issued a statement yesterday saying it is not aware of any material nonpublic information that could explain such a massive stock movement. They also said they’re going to refrain from commenting on share price speculation, only adding that it remains focused on its long-term business strategy.

You can see the exact statement in the image below. As I'll explain, based on current financials and the stock movement we've seen so far, I think investors who held TechCreate stock prior to this week should look to take the impressive profits. I would not advise anyone to buy at current levels.

First-half 2025 financial results

To even begin to understand the recent stock movement, I think it's worth taking a pretty in-depth look at their financials. In the first half of FY25, TechCreate reported just modest revenue growth but actually a pretty sharp improvement in profitability. You see, total revenue increased only 10.4% to S$1.94 million, or about US$1.51 million, from S$1.75 million in the prior-year period. This was driven mostly by higher professional services revenue from a new Real-Time Engine project they’re undergoing in Singapore. The revenue boost was also partially due to some system enhancements for an existing Brunei customer and also new hardware sales tied to its QR Soundbox Terminal business in Cambodia.

However, the cost of revenue managed to fall a hefty 22.7% to S$571,893, down from S$740,039, which I think is a reflection of lower third-party software licensing costs. This cost drop managed to boost gross profit to S$1.36 million from S$1.01 million and also lifted their gross margin to 70.5%, up rather sharply from 57.8% a year earlier. Operating expenses stayed largely flat at S$1.31 million, compared with S$1.28 million last year, although there were some higher administrative and selling expenses, which were offset by reduced R&D spending.

As a result, the company posted a very attractive-looking operating profit of S$55,715, compared with an operating loss of S$266,104 in the prior period. They also reduced their net loss dramatically to just S$20,146 (US$15,791) from S$353,918 a year earlier. In terms of the balance sheet, cash and cash equivalents came in at about S$1.3 million (US$1.0 million) as of June 30th last year, which showed us some improved operating cash flow alongside continued loan repayments.

How does TechCreate make money?

At its core, TechCreate is a technology consultancy and advanced software solutions provider. Now, they're mainly focused on digital payments and have some ongoing operations in the financial tech sector, such as helping institutional clients modernize and secure their transaction infrastructure. As per their website, the company delivers real-time payment systems, digital banking platforms, API management, cybersecurity, cloud computing, and related consulting and integration services. Their main clients seem to be financial institutions, telecommunications companies, deposit insurers, and other enterprises, mainly across Asia.

If we look into their exact offerings, they have a proprietary Artificial Intelligence Real-Time Engine (AI-RTE), which is tipped to generate a decent chunk of their revenue. Now, this is the software platform behind its payment/transaction systems. It can process financial transactions instantly and securely, and the company has already taken the step of introducing artificial intelligence to optimize things like speed, accuracy, and fraud detection. So essentially, AI-RTE is what runs their real-time payments network and makes up most of their payment offerings.

As for revenue, it comes from several streams, including professional services, so implementing tech like real-time engines, software licenses/maintenance, hardware solutions, and hosting/support services. From the first half of the FY25 release, professional services were a key revenue driver, which saw a boost from a new Real-Time Engine project in Singapore. However, there aren’t any disclosed detailed specifics that I could find, such as the exact customer or contract value. The same is true for the revenue contributions from system enhancements for that existing customer in Brunei. Elsewhere, hardware sales were said to grow via their QR Soundbox Terminal business for a Cambodian bank. The QR Soundbox is a payment hardware solution that basically allows merchants to accept QR-code-based cashless payments with just audio/visual confirmation done in real-time.

TCGL stock valuation

TechCreate stock is currently trading at about $203, up 889% yesterday and another 135% in pre-market on the 30th of January. It’s clear the stock is going a bit parabolic, and that price is now reflective of some extreme volatility rather than fundamentals. Its market cap is sitting at about $1.76 billion, with a total debt figure of $573,860. Their cash sum sits right around $1.03 million, which gives us an enterprise value roughly equal to the market cap.

The way I see it, the traditional valuation metrics are essentially meaningless now because of the stock’s sudden surge. If you glance at the valuation page, you can see that the trailing twelve-month price to sales ratio sits at 584.7x, and the price to book is at 2,199x. These are multiples well beyond the sector medians, which sit in the 3x to 4x range. Enterprise value multiples are similarly distorted, as you would expect. The EV to sales is at 682x, and EV to EBITDA and EV EBIT are, for the most part, non-meaningful since the company only has minimal earnings.

Now, these metrics are so out of proportion because of the share price swing. And I wouldn’t advise any investor to consider buying into TCGL stock at current levels. It seems like we are yet to find out what has been driving the share price, but I don’t think it is the company's performance. TechCreate’s actual revenue is modest at about US$1.5 million for the first half of 2025, and net profits are near breakeven.

In terms of a technical look at the stock, the RSI is at 99.16, which just goes to show you how extremely overbought the conditions now are. The shares have also traded between a 52-week low of $3.95 and a high of $254. Year to date, the stock has skyrocketed 4,800%, including a 2,900% increase in the past five days alone.

Final thoughts

Just to give my final thoughts, since I don't want to speculate as to what may have caused this price swing, I think TechCreate operates in a legitimate fintech niche with real products and customers, but the recent stock behavior is being driven by extreme volatility rather than business fundamentals. There is obviously no clear catalyst identified for the surge, and valuation metrics now look wildly disconnected from the kind of revenue we saw on the last earnings. So with the stock now looking so overpriced at current levels, I would advise investors to refrain from buying at these prices. Especially with the lack of clarity around what triggered the move.

data.title: Apple: Record iPhone Revenue, But There's Much More To The Bullish Story
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) shares were little changed following strong fiscal Q1 results posted on Thursday night, January 29. Its iPhone sales were particularly strong to finish off 2025, which may have actually provided a better tell on the consumer writ large rather than on Apple’s immediate future. Specifically, iPhone revenue rose 23% YoY to $85 billion, led by Pro model demand and a recovering China market, where revenue surged 38%. Services revenue was +14% YoY.

What’s more, the company announced that is will prioritize the launch of premium iPhones this year, so it’s clear where the focus is... amid a sector that’s all about AI.

I had a buy rating on Apple back in the middle of 2024. Shares are up a solid 20% since then, though that has lagged the S&P 500 (SP500) by about 8 percentage points. Today, I reiterate a Buy rating. The high-quality Tech-sector stalwart is on track with profit growth, while technicals are generally positive. I’ll provide an updated valuation and a refreshed look at the chart.

AAPL Lagging Tech Sector & S&P 500 YoY

In January, Apple reported a very strong set of quarterly results. Q1 GAAP EPS of $2.84 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $2.67, while revenue of $144 billion, up 16% from the same period a year earlier, was a large $5.2 billion beat. It was a record quarter for Cupertino, with all-time high net sales in both iPhone and Services. On the call, Tim Cook and the management team projected 13-16% top-line growth YoY for the March quarter.

Shares were fractionally lower in the pre-market trade on Friday, January 30. If that decline holds to the end of the session, it would mark the sixth straight negative earnings reaction. The options market had priced in a moderate 4.3% earnings-related stock price swing, while implied volatility coming into the print was just 29.6%--that should fall now that the Q1 numbers are posted. The next dividend on the $3.8 trillion market cap stock comes on February 10.

Record iPhone sales drove the best-ever revenue gain. iPhone 17 has been a sleepy winner in terms of the overall refresh cycle, as the street remains fixated on AI monetization. So, why did the stock trade down a bit? Costs were in focus, particularly in its cloud unit. Apple may be forced to hike prices, which always leads to uncertainty as to where both retail and enterprise customers may go.

The big question is, “Is Apple just an iPhone company?” Of course, iPhone will always be the heart of the company, but investors may not give the company credit for that—the stock has lagged since late last year. The upside catalyst is if the firm can roll out an effective AI platform with its biggest asset—the huge install base.

Right now, it’s clear a balancing act between investing (spending) on capex, while being prudent with capital... and that’s where Apple shines. It has a history of not being first to the market on leading evolutions, but biding their time, then pouncing at the right moment. I think that could be the playbook with AI this year.

iPhone Sales Surged in Q4

As it stands, Apple begins 2026 with huge iPhone demand, record gross margins, and several upcoming stock catalysts. Amid the successful iPhone upgrade cycle (particularly out of China), Services helped the company to tally a 48.2% gross margin... despite a $1.4 billion tariff hit.

On the call, the management team guided March-quarter margins to 48–49% and appears well-positioned to manage component cost pressures. Key growth drivers ahead include the launch of an AI-enabled Siri, a foldable iPhone expected in September 2026, and a record installed base of 2.5 billion devices fueling double-digit Services growth.

As for the earnings outlook, EPS growth is seen consistent and strong in 2026 and through the out year. We could be looking at more than $10 in operating EPS by FY 2028, and the balance sheet strength and near 10% long-term earnings growth rate warrant a premium valuation.

The sellside, meanwhile, remains upbeat, as evidenced by a high 30 bottom-line upgrades compared to just 7 EPS downgrades. I expect more upgrades following the encouraging Q1 report. Finally, profitability trends are very strong—free cash flow per share is $6.61, a more than 2% FCF yield.

Apple: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates from my previous analysis, some 18 months ago. If we assume normalized non-GAAP EPS of $8.70, near the current consensus, and apply a 31x multiple, then shares should trade near $270. Also keep in mind that the current forward PEG ratio is somewhat attractive at just 1.53x.

AAPL: Fair to Slightly Undervalued, Modest PEG

Key risks for Apple include a potential slowdown in the iPhone cycle due to weaker consumer spending, a near-term deceleration in Services growth (particularly in the App Store and Licensing), and possible YoY declines in gross profit.

The currently weakening dollar (DXY) has been a boon, but a dollar boost could hurt net profits due to its multinational exposure. Of course, higher tariffs and a broader trade war is an immediate challenge in what is already a highly competitive tech hardware and software industry. Steep commodity prices can’t go unnoticed, too, given the huge runup in input costs.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Thursday, April 30 AMC. Before that, shares trade ex a $0.26 dividend on Monday, February 9.

The company’s big annual shareholder meeting takes place on Tuesday, February 24.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With an all-around solid end to CY 2025, Apple’s technical situation is mixed, but there are major red flags. Notice in the chart below that shares put in an all-time high of $289 last December, then corrected to $243 earlier this month. That move on its own is not overly worrisome, but it came as the broader market rallied, resulting in significant negative alpha. Still, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

For now, resistance is seen near $260, while the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is in a bearish zone between 20 and 60. I’d like to see $260 reclaimed, and I think that’s likely, given that AAPL retraced 38.2% (Fibonacci) of the April 2025 to December 2025 rally.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph—share bounced right where heavy previous volume entered the picture. So, there should be support if we see a continued pullback.

Readers may also notice a potential head-and-shoulders top. That first broke down through $265, triggering a $242 target—and that was achieved almost to the dollar. $243-$244 is now the next possible neckline that’s critical to hold.

AAPL: Key $243 Support, $260-$265 Resistance, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on AAPL. I see the Magnificent Seven stalwart as decent enough on valuation, sporting a strong balance sheet with best-in-class management, while the chart supports the case for a rebound.

data.title: Murphy Oil: Building On The Successful Appraisal Well
data.description:
The last article on Murphy Oil (MUR) covered the successful appraisal well in Vietnam that made a discovery look a whole lot better. This overshadows the latest earnings report. But there are still two more wells to be drilled to define the field and get an idea of its production capability. As of now, Vietnam looks like it is going to be a significant part of the company, probably beginning in 2030. There is a discovery that should begin production in the current year. But that appraisal well would appear to be part of a discovery with much larger production capabilities. It likely will be adding a lot of reserve value to the company in the future.

Appraisal Well

The appraisal well confirmed a good deal more oil to produce than was originally thought to be the case with the discovery. As a result, the potential reserves are possibly closer to the high end of the original range.

This success overshadowed the earnings due to its significance. Now, as management mentioned in the conference call, the Vietnam business will likely have more production than the Eagle Ford in roughly five to ten years.

There are things not really included in the slide above, like the shallow reservoir that was discovered with the second well. That shallow reservoir could add materially to the oil in place (that is recoverable) estimate shown on the slide.

There are going to be two more wells drilled in the current fiscal year. That should add substantially to the knowledge. Management mentioned an FID in 2027 (hopefully), with production three to four years after that.

Note that the stock price really did not respond much to the announcement of the appraisal well's success. There is every chance that the stock price will move upwards as the time for first production moves closer.

Lac Da Vang

This project in Vietnam is further along, with first oil expected towards the end of the fiscal year.

This development project is now far smaller than the field that the appraisal well was drilled in. Nonetheless, having cash flow sooner rather than later is probably something that the market may well respond to.

There is an exploration well that, if successful, could be tied to this production operation as well. More will be known about that possibility in the future.

Looking at both projects in total, Vietnam took a step towards being roughly one-quarter of the company's production in the future.

As management mentioned during the conference call, the money spent here will likely be spent "no matter what happens with the price of oil". It is just that profitable (the way things look right now). More will be known when the results of two more wells are known. But for right now, this project became unexpectedly promising in a major way.

As a result, the Vietnam business far overshadowed current earnings.

Exploration Program Summary

The exploration program for Africa and Vietnam is summarized below for fiscal year 2026.

Murphy has had a surprising amount of success offshore so far in the fiscal year. It will be a while before any of this is produced. But there are clearly some decent growth possibilities from the exploration program so far.

Expenditures

One of the reasons for the relative optimism about the budget being maintained is that the balance sheet is in a particularly good spot. This does allow some flexibility if something unexpected happens with commodity prices.

Highlights of the capital plan are shown below:

Note that there is one more Vietnam well planned than this article has discussed so far. The other two wells are higher-risk exploration wells in that they are testing areas with relatively little activity recently but that have decent prospects. Even if the worst happens and the Côte d’Ivoire program fails completely (and comes up empty-handed) there is a very good chance that the latest appraisal well probably added a whole lot more potential recoverable value than can be lost in the Côte d’Ivoire program. That appraisal well was so good that it may have made the exploration and appraisal program a big success all by itself.

Earnings

Murphy's quarterly earnings are regularly overshadowed by the exploration and appraisal program. Murphy is a company that has built a solid balance sheet with the idea that the company will look for elephants that materially improve the company's business.

The longer-term strategy is to grow the business slowly while keeping a strong balance sheet. A fair amount of "would be earnings" is used in exploration for potential "home runs" or elephants. This causes earnings comparisons to be very volatile.

That interest rate shown above for the latest financing deal demonstrates just how much financial flexibility this company has. Plus, there is really not any material debt due for some time.

Therefore, the company has the financial flexibility to pursue the Vietnam development and exploration strategy under a wide variety of commodity price projections.

Cash flow easily supports the debt due even though the debt due is not due for some time to come. Quarterly income can vary widely because these offshore wells are relatively expensive, and Murphy will write off a dry hole right away.

So quarterly comparisons tend to be lumpy. Similarly, the offshore wells can be quite large. Growth can therefore be lumpy due to Murphy's relatively small size for the offshore business.

Note that management is guiding to initially lower production for 2026, as any production additions are not expected until the second half of the fiscal year. But this is not unusual when the production additions are fairly large offshore wells.

Much of the initial decline is expected from a natural gas field in Canada. But the decline there is expected to be offset by stronger natural gas prices. This should mute any initial cash flow decline somewhat.

Conclusion

There is every chance that the successful appraisal well added roughly $1 billion of recoverable reserves. That assumes it added about 200 million barrels of oil (in the ground) worth $5 to the company. That is an extremely rough estimate that could go up or down considerably as the company continues to evaluate the situation. Note also that the $1 billion includes nothing from the upper reservoir yet, as management has not yet gotten enough of a handle to guess at the upper reservoir that was encountered.

Clearly, the Vietnam business took a big step forward. That overshadowed the normal earnings report in a big way. There are a whole lot more possibilities in Vietnam. But it would not take a whole lot more progress for Murphy to be a very different company in the 2030s than it is today.

This is a very strong buy idea that will get off to a slow growth start until the business in Vietnam "gets going" when it comes to production. But it does look like the "gets going" will be substantial and extremely profitable as well.

The company has upside potential elsewhere as well. But right now, this company finally has a decent sized field to develop. Hopefully there will be more of these fields in the future.

Risks

Even given the appraisal well, the offshore part of the industry is notoriously unpredictable. It is therefore possible that the Vietnam business could disappoint management in the future without notice. That possibility is dimming as the appraisal program proceeds. But disappointments are a routine part of the offshore business.

The balance sheet strength has always been a priority here. A departure from that strategy should bring about a complete re-evaluation of the company's prospects.

Even after discoveries, appraisals, and FIDs happen, there is the actual project itself to get to production. Cost overruns or other issues could undo the potential profit of the program. An oil leak offshore can prove to be expensive in so many ways.

data.title: Pan American Silver: More Risk Than Reward, Opposite Of Early 2025 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Regular readers know I am one of the true contrarians out of the independent-thinking authors on Seeking Alpha. I call it like I see it, even if such upsets followers. I have a decent gold bug following, mostly writing bullish articles on the major miners and explaining the long-term undervaluation of precious metals generally for many years.

However, the incredible and out-of-control nature of precious metals gains, especially in Silver (XAGUSD:CUR), over the past 3-6 months should cause any contrarian to rethink the bullish future a little.

I have written five or six downgrade articles on other miners since the autumn, some back to Hold, others to downright Sell. To me, Coeur Mining (CDE) here and NovaGold (NG) here are ones to avoid in your portfolio until silver and Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) come back down to earth. Of course, you can argue "this time is different" for hard-money assets (plenty of advisors and analysts are pushing this concept today), but you are absolutely walking out further and further on a skinny tree limb. Eventually, it may break, taking your portfolio back to the ground (maybe even emotionally and physically injuring yourself in the process).

The last time precious metals went parabolic was 1979-80, culminating in the government and COMEX busting the infamous Hunt Bros. attempt to corner the silver market. If I wanted to scare those clamoring and hyperventilating to jump into precious metals [PM] and related miners now, remember early 1980's peak pricing event in U.S. dollars would last until 2008 for gold and 2011 for silver (if not 2025). Without a dividend component, it took "decades" to get back your original investment. [Note: Charted metals pricing back to 1960 below does not include January trading action.]

I will say, after an expected 6 to 12-month correction (which could prove quite rapid and severe for percentage change), I may transition back to holding a decent-sized precious metals position. The U.S. sovereign debt issue and likely continued sharp devaluation of the dollar are problems not going away. But to me, the investment risk side of the equation has grown quite massive. Sure, another 10% or 20% of straight-up gains are possible in gold in silver. Yet, I can find dramatically better risk/reward investment setups elsewhere today. Why not focus on them?

Anyway, another silver-focused miner I would Avoid and/or Sell currently is Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS). My last article on the company was a super-bullish rating posted in March 2025 here.

PAAS has experienced an amazing run over the past 52 weeks from $20 to $70. Deservedly so, "IF" silver and gold could stick the landing and remain above US$4500 and $60 respectively per ounce, many years into the future. I have serious doubts this will be our reality, trading precious metals since 1986.

Let's review some of my background logic for turning bearish on Pan American Silver, after a once-every-decade (or two) run higher in price.

Valuation Not Attractive

Pan American Silver is NOT a great candidate for silver/gold investors today if you look at forward earnings estimates. Most other major miners have longer-life reserves and resources at their mines priced at cheaper P/Es. This is a valuation idea I like to harp on.

17.5x EPS for a 2027 forward valuation estimate is way more expensive than leading global miner Newmont (NEM) for example. Why? Because each miner is its own valuation animal, with differing variables. 17.5x EPS (optimistically predicted) on 15 years of economic reserves/resources for PAAS (my guesstimate, excluding the Escobar asset under care and maintenance, up to 25 years if reopened) is not the same "value" as NEM's 13.6x P/E on likely 30+ years of identified reserves. One is fairly priced to overvalued when accounting for nation-location risk for mines (almost entirely Mexico, Guatemala and South America in the case of PAAS), while the other still remains quite undervalued (giving shareholders a margin-of-safety cushion if you will). Under bearish assumptions (which may or may not play out), where execution is lacking, mining costs and production levels are overstated by management, or silver/gold come crashing down, Pan American would get hit pretty hard for price.

Plus, projections for earnings are heavily influenced by analyst expectations metals pricing will remain elevated during 2026-27. What if PM quotes quickly slide lower without warning? Let's say interest rates rise dramatically in 2026 instead of fall, as conventional wisdom has been trained to believe. Unexpectedly higher oil/gas pricing this year could bring both higher inflation (cost pressures for miners) alongside a bump up in long-term interest rates. Such an outcome could translate into a material drop in income with a lower valuation re-rating on Pan American Silver's operating results (a double-whammy of sorts).

What I am saying is a drop in gold/silver would crush current income estimates, while a P/E closer to 10x would be about the same core "value" projection as 13.6x for Newmont (after adjusting for known silver/gold in the ground). 10x EPS of $2.50 would be equal to $25 per share, not $63 today to generate an "undervalued" position for Pan American Silver (worst-case scenario).

The total enterprise value to forward 1-year sales projections (2027) for PAAS is also stretched at one of the highest readings in its peer group at 6x. Those with even higher valuations of total equity and net debt vs. sales have either higher-quality reserves with lower cost structures to mine or safer jurisdiction risk for asset locations (or a combination).

Seeking Alpha's unbiased Quant Factor Valuation tool sorts all common equities in the gold/silver mining industry and spits out some basic relative results, using financial ratio analysis. Pan American Silver gets a "D" Grade currently. Compared to peer group averages and the company's 5-year trading history, there exist smarter choices in the sector to consider for purchase.

Other Thoughts

Over periods of a year or two, gold/silver bullion and related miners can be quite volatile and cyclical for price swings. If you are new to PM investing, buying low and selling high are of the utmost importance. No doubt about it, the January 2026 price point is incredibly stretched for silver/gold assets (industrywide averaging 2.5x the total cost of steady production rates for gold, 4x for silver, the way I calculate it). We are the most overbought since January 1980.

I do believe the long-term trend for monetary metals will continue to be tilted in an upward direction until the U.S. dollar is no longer a fiat currency and/or we can balance our lopsided federal budget and international trade deficits with the world. Neither is going to play out anytime soon, perhaps not in our lifetimes. However, PM do not move in a straight line up forever.

The U.S. dollar has been devalued by greater than -99% since we left a gold standard (convertibility for central banks) in the early 1970s under President Nixon, with gold rising from US$50 to $5,500 an ounce this week (over 55 years). It's the reason all payments to the federal government were required to be made in currencies backed by gold and silver, outlined in the U.S. Constitution (Article 1. Section 10.). The framers of this document and fathers of our nation wanted nothing to do with fiat currencies or a central bank originally. Paper currencies with no backing had led to disaster for empires and nations 100% of the time throughout history going into the late 1700s (as rising inflation becomes inevitable, stealing wealth).

It's been my argument for decades, leaving the gold standard was a serious error for America's growth-engine economy. The lack of hard-money restraints on deficit spending by the government has been the primary reason the U.S. has not had honest economic growth since at least 1999-2000, in my humble estimation (with inflation systematically understated since the 1990s). For statistical proof, unproductive gold and silver buried in your back yard have "outperformed" popular S&P 500 ETF (SPY) investing since 2001 (including dividends) or even Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

I will stop my rant there. Explaining the truth doesn't help the country much at this late stage of money printing lunacy. Back to Pan American.

Investment Conclusion

How could Pan American get to $100 per share this year or next? Well, for starters gold/silver quotes need to stay in the stratosphere.

Second, PAAS must find a way to get its awesome, low-cost mining operation in Guatemala reopened. The Escobal Mine shut down for local environmental reasons in 2017 needs to return to production mode. It would be a huge support for income growth at PAAS and bring in tens of millions in tax receipts annually for the Guatemalan government (plus create hundreds of jobs).

Nevertheless, if Escobal remains closed and gold/silver prices plummet later in 2026, Pan American Silver could reverse into a significant downtrend for shareholders. Losses of -50% or greater are a legitimate possibility from $63.

If upside is limited to +50% in a best-case scenario during the next 12 to 24 months, while -50% losses seem to have better odds of happening, what's the point owning PAAS?

Insiders and management have been heavy sellers of the stock in January. They feel now is the time to exit shares and cash in gains. Why disagree with them?

Without doubt, extremes in volatility day-to-day and intraday is now part of the silver/gold trading equation, as the market tries to digest the rare spike in quotes since the summertime.

I am downgrading my Pan American Silver rating from Buy to Sell with this article, using a 12-month outlook. Approaching downside for the precious metals cycle could be quite painful for late-to-the-game buyers. Food for thought anyway.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Pfizer: Buyer Beware, The Risk Outweighs The Reward
data.description:
There is no doubt, we are currently in the midst of an incredible bull run.

The past 3 years have simply been amazing.

  • 2023: +26.3%

  • 2024: +25.0%

  • 2025: 17.9%

It's times like these where investors love to flaunt the returns they've generated on their various positions, and there are plenty of large cap 'growth stocks' that have done quite well that most investors are exposed to, often through index funds. In fact, those who have performed best during prolonged bull markets are often those investing into the highest beta names. The issue with this is these are the investors that also typically perform the worst during bear markets.

Why do I bring this up? Because it is incredibly important we ground ourselves in the reality that investing must always be looked at through the lens of risk adjusted returns. And unfortunately in the case of Pfizer (PFE), despite the fact they are trading well below their historic average valuation multiples and below their sector averages, they have a glaring issue that most seem to be looking over. This is a flaw significant enough that it alone disqualifies Pfizer (and any other company struggling with this issue) from consideration as a potential investment for me.

That issue is Capital Allocation. As a result, Pfizer will be looking at a weakening balance sheet, no share buybacks (despite the company trading at a historically low valuation), limited growth opportunities, and even potentially risking their their status as a consistent dividend grower.

Pfizer currently yields close to 7%, placing it firmly in high-yield territory. Wall Street consensus and Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating both remain broadly constructive, with the average analyst price target implying roughly 10% upside from current levels.

At first glance, the setup looks compelling. We're talking about a blue chip pharmaceutical company trading near decade-low valuations, that has historically grown its dividend nearly every year, offering an elevated dividend yield.

But again, after examining Pfizer’s dividend sustainability, capital allocation priorities, pipeline outlook, and valuation, I believe the risk-adjusted return profile remains unattractive.

Capital Allocation

I've long considered the most important task of management to be capital allocation. Ultimately, everything a company does and how they reward shareholders flows from how effectively management deploys free cash flow. This is why free cash flow (more specifically, free cash flow per share) is the ultimate measure of intrinsic value. At a high level, every dollar of free cash flow can only be used in one of five ways:

  1. Reinvest back into the business
  2. Pay down debt
  3. Pursue mergers and acquisitions
  4. Repurchase shares
  5. Pay dividends

Strong management is able to properly allocate capital into these groups in a way that maximizes shareholder value. Weak capital allocators are eventually forced into trade-offs that hurt shareholders. Unfortunately, Pfizer currently finds itself in the latter camp, and in a way that is starting to spiral out of control.

The Dividend Is Eating the Business

Pfizer pays an annual dividend of roughly $1.72 per share, which translates into a yield just under 7% at today’s price. Obviously, that is an attractive yield, and Pfizer has been growing that dividend for 15 years in a row now. In 2024, Pfizer generated roughly $9.8 billion in free cash flow while paying out approximately $9.5 billion in dividends. That equates to a free cash flow payout ratio of nearly 97%.

In other words, almost all of Pfizer’s free cash flow is being used simply to maintain the dividend. This of course means there is no excess capital to reinvest in growth, reduce debt in a meaningful way, or repurchase shares all of which are critical for long-term shareholder value creation. And in the case of Pfizer, this leaves them in an incredibly difficult situation.

No Buybacks at the Worst Possible Time

One of the clearest symptoms of Pfizer’s capital allocation problem is its inability to repurchase shares.

As Pfizer highlighted in their earnings presentation outlook for 2026, despite trading near a decade-low valuation and at a single-digit earnings multiple, management has stated they have no plans to repurchase shares in 2026. This of course is not by choice. With 97% of their capital being used to payout dividends, they simply cannot buy back shares without weakening the balance sheet. What is ironic about this is the fact that share buybacks are most effective when a company is undervalued. This also happens to often be when companies are least able to perform them. Pfizer simply does not have the financial flexibility to take advantage of its own depressed valuation.

A Weakening Balance Sheet

At the same time, management has emphasized its desire to deleverage the balance sheet as highlighted in the image above from their investor presentation. Reducing debt is a reasonable objective, particularly after Pfizer’s aggressive acquisition activity during the COVID era. But here’s my issue with it:

You cannot simultaneously use nearly all of your free cash flow to pay dividends, materially reduce debt, and reinvest for future growth. That is not my opinion, it is simple math. Unless we see an explosion of growth in free cash flow, Pfizer’s only realistic options to fund reinvestment are to either take on additional debt, issue new equity, or reduce the dividend. None of these outcomes are favorable for current shareholders. So the question for Pfizer is this: Will free cash flow grow substantially over the next couple of years so te can meet all of their stated capital allocation goals? The short answer is... probably not. For reference, here is the projected EPS growth for Pfizer over the next few years.

In fact, earnings are projected to decline, which of course very likely means a decline in free cash flow. The long answer for whether free cash flow can grow looks more like this:

Pharma growth is driven almost entirely by research and development. A strong and continuously replenished drug pipeline is the only way to offset patent expirations and sustain earnings over time. When analyzing pharmaceutical companies, everything eventually comes back to the pipeline. Future drugs determine future revenues, and future revenues determine whether dividends, buybacks, and balance sheet strength are sustainable.

As of late 2024, Pfizer reported approximately 101 drug candidates in its pipeline:

  • 37 in Phase 1

  • 30 in Phase 2

  • 30 in Phase 3

  • 4 in registration

Of course, a large pipeline alone does not necessarily guarantee growth. What matters is how much commercially meaningful revenue that pipeline can generate, and how quickly it can replace declining legacy products. This is where I have my concerns. In the recent earnings report, management disclosed that roughly $17 billion in revenue will be exposed to patent expirations by the end of the decade. To put that into perspective, Pfizer generated approximately $63 billion in revenue in 2024.

This helps explain why analyst growth expectations remain weak despite a seemingly large pipeline. The pipeline is adding new revenue streams, but it is essentially just replacing the lost revenues from expiring patents. Replacing $17 billion in high-margin branded revenue is not easy, especially since there is already such little capital to reinvest back into the business. This is why growth expectations are so low. The current pipeline is set to simply replace the coming lost revenues, and their current capital allocation plan leaves very little room for reinvestment without weakening the balance sheet. However, there is one opportunity Pfizer has the ability to potentially take advantage of.

The Obesity Opportunity

One potential bright spot is Pfizer’s effort to enter the obesity drug market. This is a massive and fast-growing space, with hundreds of millions of patients worldwide and strong long-term demand. However, the timing matters. The immediate beneficiaries of the obesity market are largely set to be Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO). As management has stated, even if Pfizer succeeds, meaningful revenue contribution from obesity treatments is unlikely until later in the decade. On top of this, it is incredibly difficult to project out pharmaceutical revenues beyond five years. The level of uncertainty in future cash flows in this business segment makes it difficult to value in any sort of meaningful way.

Valuation

One of the best ways to value a high yielding stock is with a dividend discount model. However, when looking at a situation where the dividend is in question, I believe it makes sense to use a reverse DDM, essentially asking how much dividend growth is currently priced into the stock. In the case of Pfizer, we can see the market is currently pricing in 1.79% dividend growth to get to its current share price.

Due to their capital allocation woes, Pfizer stated that there goal is to now 'maintain the dividend'. In other words, it is not likely the dividend will be growing in 2026, and potentially for years to come. When it comes to valuation, I thin it is fair to say this means there is more downside for Pfizer. In fact, if we assumed 0% dividend growth for Pfizer moving forward, the stock would be worth just $20.24. This would imply 22.47% downside.

This leads me to believe the market is already pricing in more of a 'best case scenario', despite the fact Pfizer won't be growing its dividend in the near future while also seeing negative earnings growth and battling their capital allocation woes.

Final Thoughts

I'm still always amazed by the amount of investors looking for yield who turn to stocks that have historically grown dividends, but are now growing dividends at the expense of the overall business. Pfizer has been one of those stocks over the last few years. Pfizer may look optically cheap relative to their historic valuation multiples while providing a nice yield, but the reality is that nearly all of its free cash flow is already spoken for. The dividend is no longer a reward for shareholders. It has instead become a constraint on a business that desperately needs to heavily invest into research and development while also deleveraging. Upcoming patent expirations are adding fuel to the fire and will soon lead to $17 billion in lost revenue, leading to negative earnings growth projections from analysts. While a successful pivot into obesity or oncology could improve the outlook over time, investors today are being asked to accept significant risk for modest and uncertain reward. Again, we must always look at stocks from a risk to reward perspective, and when looking at Pfizer through this lens, it is clear Pfizer is not the type of business I want to own in it's current state.

data.title: Now Is Not The Time To Be Bearish On The U.S. Economy
data.description:
Slow but steady

In 2025, economic growth in the US slowed modestly but remained resilient. Despite the largest tariff shock in a century, fiscal contraction, stock market crash and QT, the US economy still managed to grow around 2-3%. A perfectly average year.

Of course, the distribution of resilience among sectors and cohorts was not even. In particular, 2025 was relatively weak from a manufacturing and construction perspective. It was resilience in consumption and a lack of layoffs that helped keep the economy afloat.

For much of the past few years, manufacturing PMIs have been weaker than services PMIs. This was true again in 2025.

Having said that, Manufacturing PMIs have not been at recessionary levels when using a composite of the ISM and S&P data (a series that tends to have a higher correlation to actual hard data than either of the series on their own). Again, we have seen weakness in cyclical sectors, but not recession-level weakness.

Overall, most economic data in 2025 to date has come in at average levels. This is a trend my Economic Stress Index captures well. Right in the middle of the park.

Survey data on the other hand has been much weaker. This is particularly true of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey. However, the NFIB’s Small Business Optimism Index has been consistently more positive, suggesting the weakness in the consumer surveys is attributable to tariff headlines and political uncertainty in addition to the widening gap between the rich and poor in the US, or K-Shaped economy, rather than actual recessionary conditions. Regardless, none of these data series have a particularly high correlation to actual underlying economic growth.

Looking now at key economic data at a more granular level, we can see further evidence that most of the headline hard data has been growing at average levels, with manufacturing data relatively weaker. As noted, employment and consumption have been much stronger. And, while both have slowed steadily in recent years, overall consumption is still coming in at average levels while employment growth is only slightly below. A lack of layoffs had also kept a lid on the unemployment rate, despite slowing job growth. The more volatile industrial production, retail trade and sales were relatively weak in 2023 and 2024 (but not at recessionary levels), and are now showing signs of modest recovery over the past quarter or two.

These more volatile data tend to be the cyclical drivers of the business cycle, and are more heavily impacted by borrowing costs and monetary policy. Despite their small overall share of the US economy, manufacturing, business investment, housing and construction are generally the cyclical divers of both the business cycle and employment cycle.

Cyclical data overall has been much weaker over the past few years than the much larger consumption and services side of the economy, but have not been weak enough to push the economy into recession. We can see below how manufacturing production and durable goods new orders spent much of 2023-2024 in contraction before showing signs of a recovery in 2025 (right as employment started to weaken). Private investment has also declined modestly but remains relatively strong.

More concerning has been the decline in construction spending and heavy truck sales, of which both data series are near recessionary levels.

The weakness in 2023, 2024 and the first half of 2025 in manufacturing and current weakness in construction has lead to a notable decline in cyclical jobs growth. However, manufacturing jobs growth appears to be bottoming as manufacturing activity is improving. The same cannot yet be said of construction employment.

The weakness in construction spending and job growth is largely being driven by a lack of activity in the housing market. For the past 18-24 months, the US housing market has effectively stalled as high mortgage rates and higher lending standards have crowded out buyers. Housing demand has declined as evidenced by weakness in new home sales, building permits and housing starts. Supply has risen and the appreciation in home prices slowed. Residential investment and housing construction activity have both contracted as a result, and thus the housing market has now become the primary drag on economic activity and employment.

On the services side of the economy, much of the resilience in consumption and overall economic growth can be attributed to the terrific shape of household balance sheets in aggregate. Debt levels and debt serviceability are at multi-decade lows, while cash balances and household net worth at all-time highs.

Overall, the US economy has been holding up well, though slowing at the margins. Job growth has been weak, but layoffs subdued. Manufacturing and construction activity have also been weak, but not at recessionary levels. We are seeing an improvement in manufacturing data, but not yet construction. Employment growth has been weak, but a lack of layoffs is keeping a lid on the unemployment rate. This economic resilience can be largely attributed to the following:

  • High income earners and the wealthiest Americans supporting consumption;

  • Multi-decade strength in household and corporate debt levels, debt serviceability, net wealth and overall balance sheets;

  • Persistent fiscal support (although this impulse weakened in 2025 thanks largely to higher tariff revenue);

  • Labour hoarding by businesses; and,

  • AI and on-shoring capital investment.

As a result of these factors, the US economy has been able to avoid recession and continue to growth at a resilient pace in the face of multi-decade highs in interest rates, inflation and tariffs, a housing market that has effectively stalled and weakness in the manufacturing sector.

The K-Economy

Much has been made of the K-Shaped US economy, so before we delve into the outlook for the US economy, it worth going over just how bifurcated things have become in the US between the have and have-nots.

Prior to COVID-19 and the extreme monetary and fiscal response to the crisis, the societal gap between income cohorts in the US was already stretched. It is now extreme. And, even though the wealth of the top 10% is supporting much of the overall US economic activity, the bottom 50% are struggling mightily with higher interest rates and lower wage growth.

The below chart illustrates the annual expenditures of US citizens by income quartile. The top 20% spend nearly as much as the bottom 80% combined.

Much of this is a result of the vast wealth the top 10% have accumulated. This wealth cohort makes up nearly 70% of total wealth in the US. These two charts go a long way to explain why consumption has been so strong over the past couple of years.

As a result, the bottom 50% are heavily reliant on debt to fund consumption and housing.

As such, higher interest rates have hurt borrowers (i.e. the bottom 50%), while it has increased interest income for those with little to no credit (i.e. the top 10%).

Home ownership in turn has been trending lower over the past couple of years.

While credit growth has also been contracting for years now as those who need credit simply can’t afford it.

The disparity in wage growth has also been stark. The bottom 25% of income earners have seen wage growth fall to its lowest levels in over a decade, while wage growth for the higher quartiles remains well above their respective pre-COVID trends.

And finally, these dynamics are only being exacerbated by a jobs market that is seeing little to no job growth outside of government and public service related sectors. There is little private sector demand for workers.

All of this is creating an economy that is almost entirely supported by the consumption of the wealthy, while tight monetary policy and high borrowing costs are constraining the majority of Americans.

Fortunately, we are unlikely to see consumption by the wealthy fall from here given the fantastic state of household balance sheets in aggregate (and because most of the spending is accounted for by the wealthy), while looser monetary policy will go a long way to assisting the less wealthy, as would a recovery in the manufacturing and construction sectors.

Lead indicators are improving

On a forward looking basis, there remains plenty of evidence to suggest the US economy will continue to prove robust, and is likely to accelerate in 2026.

Firstly, high frequency data shows Q4 real GDP growth will be around 2-2.5%, which is roughly average in recent history.

For Q4, the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow is suggesting GDP may have been as high as 5.4% on a real basis, highlighting just how far away from recession or any kind of material downturn the US economy has actually been. This number will likely come down as further Q4 data is released, but it shows just how well the US economy continues to do.

My composite lead indicator is also pointing to sideways growth over the next few months. This suggests economic robustness is likely to continue.

The Conference Board’s LEI also continues to accelerate off a low base, though it yet to move meaningfully higher. However, the correlation between the Conference Board LEI and actual economic growth has been minimal post-COVID.

As I touched on earlier, the cyclicality of the US economy is largely driven by a few sectors, notably manufacturing, investment and construction (particularly residential), much of which are intertwined. The trends in employment and consumption do matter, but even then, it tends to be employment within these cyclical sectors that drives fluctuations in overall employment, ditto with durable goods consumption driving volatility in overall consumption.

As a result, looking at leading data within these various sectors and areas of the economy provides great insight to where the US economy is headed over the medium term.

Beginning with the jobs market, much has been made of the weakness in jobs growth in recent times. But as we saw earlier, jobs growth is still at average levels, while cyclical jobs growth is contracting. This is largely a result of labour hoarding. That is, companies are not hiring new workers, but they are also not laying off existing workers.

Importantly, the outlook for the jobs market remains reasonable. Truflation’s Employment Index suggests that high frequency employment data has been improving over the past six months, which means officials jobs numbers should follow suit.

My employment leading indicator is also not pointing to further declines in cyclical or overall jobs growth, and is in fact pointing to acceleration.

In terms of consumption, there is also little reason to suggest we are likely to see consumers pull back their spending in the coming quarters.

Most important here is the wealth effect remains a tailwind to consumption and overall growth (even if it is largely being driven by the top 10%).

Financial conditions remain supportive of this dynamic.

As does CEO Confidence, though to a lesser extent.

The same can be said of household net worth, though the correlation between net worth and GDP growth has broken down somewhat post-COVID.

But, with asset prices rising and bank lending standards for consumer credit falling (inverted below), this is on net a tailwind for consumer spending.

High frequency retail sales data via the Redbook Sales Index continues to confirm consumer spending is robust and even accelerating.

The manufacturing, production and inventories cycle is where things get interesting.

As we saw earlier, durable goods orders and changes in durable goods inventories are highly cyclical and are the largest contributors the economic cyclicality outside of the housing market.

Firstly, until we see a proper recover in manufacturing PMIs, it is unlikely we see industrial production growth accelerate meaningfully. However, manufacturing PMIs are not suggesting industrial production is likely to decline from here as we close out 2025.

In terms of the outlook for the ISM manufacturing PMI, the ISM New Orders/Inventories spread is still only suggesting the PMI will move modestly higher in the short-term.

While the recent trends in the Regional Fed New Orders composite is pointing to a more favourable outlook for Manufacturing PMIs.

Easing bank lending standards for commercial and industrial loans is also a modest tailwind for industrial production over the next few months.

However, until we see the acceleration in a new inventory cycle, industrial production and manufacturing activity is likely to remain muted.

On that front, we are seeing positive signs.

ISM customer inventories are becoming increasingly depleted. This should translate into an increased level of new orders…

…and manufacturing inventories as a result.

The spread between the ISM manufacturer and customer inventories surveys are also suggesting a rise in durable goods inventories should commence in the coming months.

The resilience in consumption and retail sales in particular is also supportive of an imminent buildout in inventories.

As such, there is evidence to suggest a new cycle in inventory stockpiling could come about in 2026. We are already seeing signs of this via the improvement in manufacturing data, though the extent of any inventory buildout remains to be seen. Regardless, increasing inventory levels equals increased levels of manufacturing and production, both of which are strong cyclical tailwinds for the economy.

In terms of overall business investment and capital expenditures, it is still too early to call for a durable recovery.

While Regional Fed capex intentions are a volatile data series, this composite is not yet signaling a material pick-up in fixed investment. Concerns over tariffs are still likely having an impact on business spending.

Lending standards for small business also need to ease further from here before a durable recovery in overall investment can be called for.

Another big question mark for the US economy is the outlook for the housing market. As we saw earlier, housing activity has stalled over the past couple of years thanks to high mortgage rates crowding out potential buyers.

As a result, house prices have been largely flat over the past couple of years. This deceleration should stabilise over the next six months, as should the negative impulse in building permits and construction job growth. That said, the housing market is likely to continue be a drag on economic growth for a number of months still, and we need to see mortgage rates move lower to spur a durable recovery in the housing market.

Getting the housing market going again by lowering the policy rate is one effective tool the Federal Reserve has at its disposal to assist the bottom end of the K. As it stands, markets are only pricing in two rate cuts in 2026 and whether that is enough to spur a recover remains to be seen. Regardless, the economic impact from the housing market is unlikely to worsen from here and should improve on the margins over the coming quarters.

What we can be more certain of shifting from modest headwind to robust tailwind in 2026 is the fiscal impulse. While fiscal spending was obviously dovish this year, we did see a notable fiscal retrenchment thanks to increased tariff revenue. Now, with the Beautiful Bill set to come into effect in 2026, fiscal spending will once again become a material tailwind to the economy, with the CBO estimating a boost to growth by 0.9% in 2026.

2025 saw a tariff shock, QT, an equity market crash and fiscal retrenchment, and yet, growth is set to average around 2-3%. Now, with most of the negative impacts from tariffs behind us, a renewed fiscal impulse, improving manufacturing sector, improve leading economic indicators, a lower policy rate and QE, economic growth in 2026 could easily be 1-2% higher as a result. This is not a time to be bearish the US economy.

What is also constructive is that longer-term leading indicators also remain relatively supportive.

Oil prices tend to lead the business cycle by around 18 months and remain a tailwind.

So too the rate of change in interest rates.

Ditto the yield curve.

While my macro conditions index, which is a composite of the dollar, interest rates and commodity prices also remains largely supportive of the business cycle over the next nine months.

The only cause for concern from a long-leading indicator perspective has been the rise in ISM manufacturing prices. This is certainly something to keep an eye on, but for now the weight of evidence from long-lead indicators remains supportive of the US economy over the next 12-18 months.

Global growth also remains robust

What’s more, most leading indicators of the global business cycle are signaling a positive outlook.

Global monetary policy remains a tailwind for the business cycle as global central banks continue to ease on net.

While the diffusion index of OECD Composite Lead Indicators is also supportive of global growth.

This notion is supported by macro research firm Variant Perception, whose leading indicators for the US, China and Europe are all signaling positive outlooks.

Europe in particular is seeing constructive improvement in lead indicators. German manufacturing expectations are improving, Sweden’s manufacturing new orders are on the rise, while leading indicators of the OECD and Conference Board for Europe are showing signs of upside acceleration.

But, as has been the case for several years now, the biggest question to the global business cycle remains China. We did see signs of improvement from Chinese leading data in 2025, as evidence by the modest improvement in China’s credit impulse. But the supportive move in yields which tend to lead the credit impulse, M1 and PBOC balance sheet has reversed in recent months. Chinese lead indicators are also not showing encouraging signs of improvement.

While there is not yet enough evidence to suggest China’s economic struggles will worsen in 2026, particularly as we are likely to see further monetary easing, but the lack of a robust economic recovery in China may limit the duration of any recover in the global economy that appears likely in 2026.

Overall however, the weight of evidence continues to support a bullish outlook for the US economy and much of the global economy for 2026. In the US, the business cycle, earnings cycle, fiscal policy cycle and monetary policy cycle are all largely tailwinds. For risk assets this bodes well.

Summary, outlook and key macro notes:
